title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Ben Miller, who works as a park ranger.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Ben Miller, who works as a park ranger in a large country park. Ben, what first attracted you to this kind of work? || Guest: I enjoyed walking when I was young, but I didn't think of it as a job. I trained as a mechanic after school. Then my uncle invited me to volunteer on a wildlife project one summer. [20]I loved being outside all day and realised I wanted work that kept me close to nature[/20]. The machines in the garage suddenly seemed much less interesting. || Interviewer: What do visitors misunderstand about your job? || Guest: Some people think I spend all day looking at birds, which would be lovely. In fact, [21]a large part of my job is helping visitors use the park safely and explaining why certain areas are closed[/21]. People don't always like being told they can't walk somewhere, so you need to be calm and clear. || Interviewer: What's your favourite season in the park? || Guest: Spring is beautiful, and autumn colours are amazing, but [22]I prefer winter because the park is quieter and you notice small changes more easily[/22]. You can see animal tracks in mud, hear birds better, and repair paths without crowds around you. It can be cold, but the peaceful atmosphere makes up for that. || Interviewer: What is difficult about protecting wildlife there? || Guest: The main difficulty is not the animals; it is people's habits. [23]Visitors sometimes leave food for wildlife, thinking they are being kind, but it can make animals ill or too dependent on humans[/23]. We put up signs, but I spend a lot of time explaining the reasons face to face. Most people understand once you talk to them. || Interviewer: You also organise activities for school groups. How do you feel about that? || Guest: I used to worry about it because children ask unexpected questions. Now I enjoy it. [24]The best moments are when children who arrived looking bored suddenly become excited after finding insects or animal footprints[/24]. They stop seeing the park as just a place for a picnic and start noticing the living things around them. || Interviewer: What change would you like to see in the future? || Guest: More people are coming by car, and the car park is often full. [25]I'd like better bus links so visitors can reach the park without driving[/25]. That would reduce traffic in nearby villages and make it easier for teenagers and older people who don't have cars to come here. || Interviewer: Do you ever get tired of being outdoors in bad weather? || Guest: Of course there are mornings when I would rather stay inside, especially when it is raining sideways and my boots are already wet. But the park changes so much in different weather that I rarely feel bored. Even a grey day can be useful because you see where paths flood or where visitors need clearer signs. I also work with a small team, and we help each other keep cheerful when conditions are not ideal. That makes a big difference. Another part of the job is working with local residents, because the park belongs to them as much as to visitors. When people feel included, they report problems earlier and look after the place better.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Ben Miller, who works as a park ranger in a large country park. Ben, what first attracted you to this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I enjoyed walking when I was young, but I didn't think of it as a job. I trained as a mechanic after school. Then my uncle invited me to volunteer on a wildlife project one summer. I loved being outside all day and realised I wanted work that kept me close to nature. The machines in the garage suddenly seemed much less interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do visitors misunderstand about your job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some people think I spend all day looking at birds, which would be lovely. In fact, a large part of my job is helping visitors use the park safely and explaining why certain areas are closed. People don't always like being told they can't walk somewhere, so you need to be calm and clear.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What's your favourite season in the park?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Spring is beautiful, and autumn colours are amazing, but I prefer winter because the park is quieter and you notice small changes more easily. You can see animal tracks in mud, hear birds better, and repair paths without crowds around you. It can be cold, but the peaceful atmosphere makes up for that.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is difficult about protecting wildlife there?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The main difficulty is not the animals; it is people's habits. Visitors sometimes leave food for wildlife, thinking they are being kind, but it can make animals ill or too dependent on humans. We put up signs, but I spend a lot of time explaining the reasons face to face. Most people understand once you talk to them.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You also organise activities for school groups. How do you feel about that?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I used to worry about it because children ask unexpected questions. Now I enjoy it. The best moments are when children who arrived looking bored suddenly become excited after finding insects or animal footprints. They stop seeing the park as just a place for a picnic and start noticing the living things around them.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What change would you like to see in the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: More people are coming by car, and the car park is often full. I'd like better bus links so visitors can reach the park without driving. That would reduce traffic in nearby villages and make it easier for teenagers and older people who don't have cars to come here.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you ever get tired of being outdoors in bad weather?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Of course there are mornings when I would rather stay inside, especially when it is raining sideways and my boots are already wet. But the park changes so much in different weather that I rarely feel bored. Even a grey day can be useful because you see where paths flood or where visitors need clearer signs. I also work with a small team, and we help each other keep cheerful when conditions are not ideal. That makes a big difference. Another part of the job is working with local residents, because the park belongs to them as much as to visitors. When people feel included, they report problems earlier and look after the place better.

questions.question: Why did Ben decide to become a park ranger?

options.A: He wanted to use skills he learnt as a mechanic.
options.B: He discovered he liked working close to nature.
options.C: He had always planned to work with animals.

questions.explanation: Ben says a summer wildlife project made him realise he wanted work that kept him close to nature.

questions.question: What does Ben say is an important part of his job?

options.B: repairing equipment used in the park

questions.explanation: He says a large part of the job is helping visitors use the park safely.

questions.question: Why does Ben like winter in the park?

options.A: It is easier to notice details.
options.C: There are more animals to see.

questions.explanation: Ben says that in winter the park is quieter and you notice small changes more easily.

questions.question: What problem does Ben mention?
questions.explanation: He says visitors sometimes leave food for wildlife, which can be harmful.

questions.question: What does Ben enjoy about school visits?

options.B: showing children where to have picnics
options.C: seeing children become interested in nature

questions.explanation: Ben likes it when bored children become excited after finding insects or footprints.

questions.question: What improvement would Ben like?

options.A: better public transport to the park

questions.explanation: He says he would like better bus links so visitors can come without driving.
